title: The Diffraction Limit and Extremal Functions
speaker: Ankur Moitra
abstract: It is widely believed that the physics of diffraction imposes certain fundamental limits on the resolution of an optical system. In this work we study the diffraction limit as a statistical inverse problem in increasingly more realistic mathematical models and prove rigorous upper and lower bounds on the sample complexity of resolving closely-spaced point sources.

proof_stages.label: Off-grid super-resolution model and phase-transition theorem
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates sparse point-source recovery from low-frequency Fourier measurements, adds noise and separation, and states the sharp threshold m at least 1/delta plus 1 for stable recovery, with an exponentially hard regime below it.

proof_stages.label: Noiseless recovery by the matrix pencil method
proof_stages.summary: A rectangular complex Vandermonde matrix is used to build two measurement-populated matrices A and B whose generalized eigenvalues recover the spike phases, giving exact noiseless recovery when the frequencies are distinct.

proof_stages.label: Stability reduced to Vandermonde conditioning
proof_stages.summary: The robustness of the generalized eigenvalue step is reduced to whether the Vandermonde matrix has a controlled smallest singular value, so the noisy problem becomes a condition-number question.

proof_stages.label: Beurling-Selberg extremal functions as bandlimited preconditioners
proof_stages.summary: Beurling-Selberg majorants and minorants for signs and intervals are introduced, with their Fourier support and sharp L1 error identified as the mechanism for reweighting rows of the Vandermonde matrix.

proof_stages.label: Fourier-comb proof of singular-value bounds
proof_stages.summary: By rewriting the squared norm of a Vandermonde exponential sum using the Dirac comb, the bandlimited majorant/minorant kills all off-diagonal separated terms and yields upper and lower singular-value bounds with the m ± 1/delta constants.

proof_stages.label: First-principles optical model and historical diffraction criteria
proof_stages.summary: The discussion shifts from Donoho's Fourier model to Fraunhofer diffraction, Airy disks, photon samples, and classical Rayleigh-type heuristic limits, setting up the need for a rigorous statistical formulation.

proof_stages.label: Diffraction limit as sample-complexity phase transition
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states learning-theoretic results for mixtures of Airy disks: no true diffraction limit for constant k, but for growing k a transition between polynomial and exponential sample complexity governed by separation and Vandermonde-type conditioning.

examples.note: The speaker uses cell imaging and HIV entry to motivate why low-frequency or low-energy measurements can still be asked to reveal fine structure.

examples.name: Single star versus double star through an Airy disk
examples.note: The classical two-star thought experiment is used to show why visual separation criteria are insufficient and why the diffraction limit should instead be formulated statistically.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker reasons through the Airy-disk model for one versus two stars, describing the photon density, superposition of disks, and how resolution/sample complexity changes
